lesson.menu_desc: Open Block 4 -- Writing: the subject line, the greeting, one clear ask, and a polite close. Plus polite request forms -- 27 slides
lesson.subtitle: Aula 19 -- Inbox Zero, Writing Executive Emails
lesson.title_tag: Professor View -- Diogo Leal | Aula 19 -- Inbox Zero

listenings.text: Hi Diogo, it is Sophia. Last class you finished the whole presentation block. After a good talk, the work is not over. You send a short, clear email so the room remembers your message. Today we open a new block: writing. A great executive email has four parts. First, the subject line. Keep it short and clear, so the recipient knows the topic before they open it. Second, the greeting. Use Dear or Hi and the name. Third, the body. Make one clear ask, in one or two short lines, and use a polite request. Say could you please send the report, or would you be able to confirm the date. Fourth, the close. Use Best regards or Kind regards, and your name. Two quick tips. Keep it short. A busy manager reads on the phone, so three short lines beat one long paragraph. And be polite but direct. You can be warm and still ask for exactly what you need. Write the email like you speak: clear, calm, and human. You are ready. Talk soon.

Subject: Project Atlas, summary and next step. Dear Maria, thank you for your time in today's meeting. Here is a short summary. The migration is finished, and costs dropped by fifteen percent. I have one quick ask. Could you please confirm the budget for phase two by Friday? I will then send the full plan to the team. I attach the slides from the meeting for your records. If you have any questions, I am happy to help. Best regards, Diogo Leal, IT Project Manager, Oracle.

extra_audio.text: Diogo writes one short, clear executive email. First, he writes the subject line, so the recipient knows the topic before opening it. Then, he writes the greeting: Dear Maria. After that, he writes the body with one clear, polite ask: could you please confirm the budget by Friday. Then, he attaches the file the reader needs. Finally, he closes with Best regards and his name.
